Base directory for this skill: /Users/neo/.claude-personal2/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.7/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design se…
I'll explore the project and the referenced repo before asking quest
ouroboros interview think about to provide support of project's session history on our llm-wiki. check https://github.com/Pratiyush/llm-wiki.
I'll gather context on both repos before asking clarifying questions.

Context gathered. Here's what I found:

- **Local repo** (`/Users/neo/Developer/Projects/LLM-Wiki`, owner `ca1773130n`): a Python CLI that builds typed knowledge graphs and projects them to markdown/HTML/MCP/SQLite/etc. Already has `.agent-sessions/hermes/llm-wiki.session` markers (gitignored) and a "ouroboros" critique commit (`4b85593`: Codex graph-view critique).
- **Referenced repo** (`Pratiyush/llm-wiki`): a different project — a **Claude Code plugin** with commands (`maintainer`, `release`, `review-pr`, `triage-issue`, `wiki-all`). Useful as inspiration for command surface, not a fork.

So as I read it: you want LLM-Wiki to ingest and surface the **AI agent session history** of the host project as first-class wiki content (the "ouroboros" — the wiki documenting how the wiki itself was built).

Before going further, one clarifying question.

**Q1 — What is "session history" here?** Pick the closest:

- **A. Transcript ingestion** — read agent session logs (Claude Code `.jsonl`, Codex, Hermes session files) and surface them as a new ontology slice (Session → Turn → ToolCall → FileEdit) linked to the existing graph nodes (papers, files, claims).
- **B. Provenance overlay** — annotate existing wiki pages with "which session touched this node, when, why" (a `provenance` panel + "Show history" filter on the graph).
- **C. Timeline / activity view** — a dedicated page that replays sessions as a scrollable timeline (extending the home heatmap with click-through to session detail pages).
- **D. Reproducibility** — store the prompts/commands/diffs that produced each wiki artifact, so any node can be replayed (`llm_wiki session replay <id>`).
- **E. All of the above as one feature** — too big; should decompose into sub-projects.

Which is closest to what you have in mind? (And: are the "sessions" we're indexing Claude Code `.jsonl` logs, the existing `.agent-sessions/hermes/*.session` files, something else, or all of them?)
